result:
In the hush between heartbeats, I sometimes think the world is made of paper. Not the crisp, white ream stacked beside a printer, but the soft, foxed kind you find in the bottom of an attic trunk, smelling of mildew and forgotten birthdays. A single sheet can bear the weight of an entire childhood: the cursive name of a girl who pressed violets between pages in 1937, the rusted stain of her lip-prints where she kissed the ink good-night. Paper remembers the oils of hands, the sweat of fear, the dot of jam that once meant someone was in a hurry to get outside before June dissolved. If you hold it to the lamplight just so, you can see the fibers lifting like gooseflesh, each thread humming with a sentence it refused to forget.

I carry that trunk inside me. I think everyone does. We walk around like upright valises, latched but never locked, our hinges loosening with every mile. Somewhere on the left side of my ribcage is the smell of my grandmother’s stove: onions first, then thyme, then the metallic whisper of the storm lamp she lit during blackout drills. She would hum something half-remembered from opera, something that dissolved into the stew and later into my bloodstream. I never learned the name of the aria, but when I hear the first three notes—on any street, any stage—it’s 1943 again, and I’m barefoot on cool linoleum, stealing carrots from the pot while war crouched outside the door like a dog that hadn’t decided whom to bite.

Memory is not a ledger; it is a compost heap. We toss peelings, coffee grounds, the occasional diamond ring we meant to keep safe. Time turns it all into one dark humus, and from it grows the unlikely tomato plant that volunteers itself beside the garage, small and absurdly sweet. You didn’t plant it, yet there it is, feeding you. Once, in Kraków, I ate a tomato like that, warm from a stranger’s brick wall. The woman who lived there spoke no English, so we traded consonants the way children trade marbles, rolling them in our palms until they shone. She cut the fruit with a pocketknife, sprinkled it with sugar, and the taste was so close to the tomatoes my grandmother grew that I had to sit on the curb to keep from floating away. The woman patted my shoulder as if she understood I was mourning someone whose name I hadn’t yet learned to pronounce.

We are told to travel light, but what if heaviness is the point? The stones in one’s pocket—smooth, striped, worry-thumbed—are ballast against the hot-air balloon of the self. Without them we drift into jet streams of other people’s certainties, freeze at altitudes where the oxygen thins. I pocketed a shard of green beach glass in Haifa the morning I watched a father teach his son how to skip stones. The boy’s arm was all elbows, the stone clattering like a dropped spoon. Again, baba, again. By the tenth try the stone kissed the water three times, and the sound the father made was half-laugh, half-sob, as if joy were a small bird that might suddenly decide to migrate. I keep the glass for the moment when cynicism climbs my spine like ivy; it is cold against my palm, and I remember that three small splashes can be enough to keep a person alive for decades.

Sometimes I think we are each an unfinished letter addressed to nobody in particular. We write ourselves in spurts: Dear you, today the sky resembled a bruise I once gave myself by accident… then the phone rings, the kettle boils, the baby crawls toward the stairs, and the sentence dangles forever. The envelope is sealed with the DNA of the day—bus exhaust, a lover’s shampoo, the faint copper of cut basil—and sent out into the world anyway. Who opens it? A tired paramedic pausing on the bridge at 3 a.m.? The widower in the flat downstairs who waters your plants when you forget? Maybe the paper finally reaches the stranger who sat beside you on the tarmac for three hours, who never learned your name but remembers the way you gripped the armrest when the plane bounced, how that small shared fear felt like communion.

I want to believe the letter eventually arrives, even if the address smears in the rain. I want to believe the world is a vast sorting office where every unspoken kindness is rerouted, every half-forgiven grief filed under “pending miracles.” The clerks work overtime, sleeves rolled, humming the same unknown aria. They taste tomatoes in January and understand it is a clue. They stack our paper memories not in towers but in spirals, so the oldest touch the newest at unexpected edges, forming a Möbius strip of shared combustion. When the lights flicker, the whole structure glows like a lantern, and for one breath we can see the outline of everyone we ever loved projected onto the warehouse wall, waving, as if to say: Heavier, yes, but look how we shine.
